paper_title: Decaying HyperLogLog: Continuous-Time Cardinality Estimation with Exponential Aging
email_title: Decaying HyperLogLog: Continuous-Time Cardinality Estimation with Exponential Aging

sections.summary: This paper proposes Decaying HyperLogLog (D-HLL), a variant of HyperLogLog that aims to estimate a time-decayed notion of distinct count under exponential aging while preserving mergeability. The method stores multiple time-bucketed HLL sketches and answers queries by computing a weighted sum of per-bucket HLL cardinality estimates using exponential decay weights determined by the query time and bucket timestamps. The authors present synthetic experiments and claim improved accuracy over sliding-window HLL under exponentially decayed ground truth for moderate decay rates.
sections.strengths:
Technical novelty and innovation
The goal is timely and practically relevant: supporting continuous-time exponential decay in a mergeable distinct-counting sketch is an important problem for monitoring and analytics applications.
The design supports “any-λ” query flexibility since the same stored sketches can be reweighted for different decay parameters at query time.


Experimental rigor and validation
The authors provide an open-source implementation and attempt to measure throughput, memory, and accuracy across several data distributions and decay rates.


Clarity of presentation
The high-level motivation and the need for recency-aware distinct counting are clearly explained and compelling.
The method description is straightforward to follow at the level of data structure and basic operations (update, merge, query).


Significance of contributions
If correct and well-founded, a practical, mergeable, continuous-time decayed distinct estimator would be impactful for network telemetry, fraud detection, and real-time analytics.
sections.weaknesses:
Technical limitations or concerns
Fundamental semantic mismatch: summing independently estimated per-bucket HLL cardinalities with exponential weights double-counts keys that appear in multiple buckets, and does not correspond to a well-defined decayed distinct objective such as “sum over keys of e^{−λ(t−τ_last(key))}.” There is no formal definition of the target functional being estimated, and the proposed estimator is not justified as unbiased or consistent for any properly specified decayed-distinct quantity.
No theoretical analysis of estimator bias/variance or of bucketization error; HLL raw estimator biases and inter-bucket dependencies are ignored.
Mergeability claim is somewhat vacuous: merging per-bucket/register maxima is identical to standard HLL merging when bucket indices align; “<1e−15 error” implies only numerical summation differences, not an algorithmic property.


Experimental gaps or methodological issues
The “ground truth” for decayed cardinality is not formally defined; it is unclear whether duplicates across time count once with a recency-dependent weight (typical for decayed distinct) or multiple times. The proposed estimator clearly treats multiple occurrences of the same key in different buckets as multiple contributions, which is not distinctness.
Baselines are not well-chosen or fairly compared: sliding-window HLL is compared against an exponential-decay ground truth, predictably yielding enormous errors; no attempts are made to tune window size to approximate an exponential decay or to compare to existing time-decayed distinct/frequency frameworks (e.g., Cohen’s Laplace-transform reduction to distinct/max-distinct, SetSketch variants, or weighted-distinct sketches that could emulate decay).
Inconsistencies and suspect numbers in tables (e.g., Table 2 shows Standard HLL with ~1% error when compared to “decayed ground truth,” which is not meaningful unless the ground truth is actually non-decayed distinctness; throughput values in Tables 3 and 4 do not align; memory accounting is unclear and appears very large).
Only synthetic data are used; no evaluation on real traces where decay semantics matter most.


Clarity or presentation issues
The paper does not precisely define the decayed distinct cardinality functional being estimated (what is the contribution of a key with multiple occurrences at different times?).
The choice of bucket size and the induced discretization/approximation error are not analyzed or even reported.
Space complexity claims in Table 1 (“O(1) sketches per bucket”) are unclear/misleading—space per bucket is O(m) registers.


Missing related work or comparisons
The paper omits discussion of relevant frameworks that can support decayed or weighted distinctness: Cohen’s complement Laplace transform approach for soft-capping and time-decayed statistics; SetSketch family and related weighted distinct estimators; recent weighted cardinality sketches (e.g., QSketch) that could, in principle, encode decay via weights and maintain mergeability.
It also does not engage with improved HLL estimators (e.g., Ertl’s improved raw / ML) that are standard practice and could reduce bias in each bucket estimate.
sections.detailed_comments:
Technical soundness evaluation
The core estimator ẑC_λ(t_q) = Σ_b C(M_b)·e^{−λ(t_q−t_b)} is not justified as an estimator for a well-defined decayed distinct functional. For distinct counting, duplicates across time should not be additive; typical choices include taking the last-seen timestamp τ_last(k) for each key k and defining F_λ(t_q) = Σ_k e^{−λ(t_q − τ_last(k))}. The proposed sum over buckets will overcount any key observed in more than one bucket, since each relevant per-bucket HLL contributes toward that same key. Without a rigorous definition and derivation, the estimator is not technically sound for decayed distinctness.
No analysis is provided for bias, variance, or bucketization error. Using the raw HLL estimator per bucket and summing them with continuous weights ignores known small-/large-range biases and inter-bucket dependencies. There is also no guidance on how bucket size b should scale with λ or with desired relative error to bound discretization.
Mergeability is inherited trivially from HLL as long as bucket indices, precision p, and decay configuration align; reporting “<1e−15 error” conflates numerical accumulation error with conceptual mergeability and adds no substantive insight.


Experimental evaluation assessment
The claimed ground truth must be precisely defined. If “ground truth” sums per-occurrence decayed contributions, it is not a distinctness measure, and claims about decayed distinct cardinality are misleading. If “ground truth” uses last-seen semantics per key, then the proposed estimator is systematically biased high due to double counting across buckets. The reported relatively small error for Standard HLL (~1%) versus a “decayed” ground truth suggests ground truth may actually be non-decayed distinctness for Table 2, which conflicts with the narrative used to explain sliding-window’s “extremely high error.”
Baselines: Comparing a fixed-window method to an exponential-decay target is not meaningful unless the window size is tuned to approximate the half-life, and even then, the evaluation should frame the task as approximation of a target functional under model mismatch. More importantly, there are existing methods that get closer to the right objective (e.g., distinct/max-distinct estimators via Laplace-transform mappings, or weighted distinct sketches) that are not included.
The memory/throughput accounting appears off by orders of magnitude. With m=2048 registers per bucket, even 1 byte/register yields ~2 KB/bucket; 1.6 MB implies ~800 active buckets. The time horizon, bucket size, truncation policy, and their impact on accuracy and cost are not analyzed. Throughput figures in Tables 3 and 4 are inconsistent (e.g., 272 Kops/s vs 153 Kops/s for λ=0.01).
The accuracy degrades drastically for higher λ (59%–93% REL), suggesting the discretized approach is unsuitable unless bucket sizes are adapted to λ; this limitation is acknowledged but not remedied.


Comparison with related work (using the summaries provided)
Cohen (2016) shows how to reduce a broad class of concave sublinear statistics, including time-decayed soft-capped functions, to distinct or max-distinct sketches via the complement Laplace transform; this is directly relevant, as exponential decay is naturally expressed in this framework. The paper does not discuss or compare against such reductions, which can maintain mergeability and provide point/combination measurements with theoretical guarantees.
SetSketch (Ertl, 2021) offers a continuum between MinHash and HLL and supports ML-based estimation; it and related weighted distinct approaches (e.g., QSketch) provide tools that could potentially incorporate time decay via weights and maintain per-register maxima semantics and mergeability. These are not considered.
Improved HLL estimators (Ertl, 2017) reduce bias and are standard in practice; relying on the basic raw estimator per bucket is suboptimal and likely inflates error.
Compression-oriented HLL variants (HLLL, HBS) are less central, but they highlight that there is an active literature carefully analyzing register distributions and merge properties—any per-bucket approach should at least discuss how such advances could mitigate the substantial memory overhead here.


Discussion of broader impact and significance
If a correct and theoretically justified decayed distinct estimator were provided, the impact would be substantial. However, as currently defined, the estimator does not target a coherent decayed distinct functional, raising concerns about deploying it in practice: users would not be measuring what they intend (e.g., decayed number of unique sources) but rather a weighted sum of per-bucket distincts that double-count returning keys. The risk is that operators may make erroneous decisions based on misinterpreted metrics.
The code release is positive; however, without a precise target definition and thorough baselines, the empirical findings are difficult to trust or generalize.
sections.questions_for_authors:
What is the precise mathematical definition of the “decayed cardinality” you aim to estimate? For a key with multiple occurrences at times t1 < t2 < … < tk ≤ tq, what is its total contribution to the ground truth at tq?
If your intended functional is F_λ(tq) = Σ_k e^{−λ(tq − τ_last(k))}, please explain how summing per-bucket distinct estimates avoids double counting keys observed in multiple buckets. If not, please define the target and justify why it is the right notion of “decayed distinctness.”
How is ground truth computed in the experiments? Do you deduplicate keys per query, and if so, which timestamp is used (first seen, last seen, or another function)? This is essential to interpret Table 2 and the very large errors reported for sliding windows.
What is the chosen bucket size b and time horizon per experiment, and how many active buckets B are typically maintained? How do b and B relate to λ to bound discretization error?
Why use the raw HLL estimator per bucket instead of improved/ML estimators (e.g., Ertl 2017)? Did you evaluate whether using improved estimators reduces bias in D-HLL?
Can you provide an analysis (even approximate) of the bias/variance of your estimator as a function of m, b, λ, and data arrival patterns? In particular, quantify the double-counting bias under simple models (e.g., a Poisson process of reoccurrence per key).
Throughput and memory tables appear inconsistent (e.g., 272 Kops/s vs 153 Kops/s for similar settings). Please reconcile these numbers and detail the experimental setup (hardware, register widths, Python/NumPy overheads).
Could you compare against more suitable baselines (e.g., approximating exponential decay by an ensemble of sliding windows; Cohen-style reductions; SetSketch- or weighted-distinct-based approaches adapted to decay)?
What synchronization assumptions are required for mergeability in distributed settings (aligned bucket boundaries, clock skew tolerance)? How sensitive is the method to misalignment?
sections.overall_assessment:
Estimated Score:
                    2.4/10
                    (Calibrated to ICLR scale)
                
            The paper tackles an important and underexplored problem—mergeable, continuous-time decayed distinct counting—but the proposed estimator is not well-founded for the intended objective. Summing per-bucket HLL estimates with exponential weights does not estimate a coherent decayed distinct functional and systematically double-counts keys appearing in multiple buckets. The paper does not provide a formal definition of the target quantity, theoretical analysis of the estimator, or fair and convincing experimental comparisons to relevant alternatives. Several empirical results appear inconsistent or are difficult to interpret given the missing ground-truth specification, and the memory/throughput tradeoffs are unfavorable without a clear accuracy guarantee.
I encourage the authors to (1) precisely define the decayed distinct functional (e.g., last-seen semantics), (2) derive or adopt an estimator that correctly targets that functional (consider Laplace-transform-based reductions to distinct/max-distinct sketches, or a per-register timestamp design with proper analysis), (3) analyze bucketization error and the bias/variance tradeoffs, (4) incorporate improved HLL estimators per bucket, and (5) conduct thorough, fair comparisons including real-world data. In its current form, the paper falls short of ICLR standards for technical soundness and empirical validation.
